---
title: "Version control"
---

# Fundamentals

To further improve the reproducibility of an analysis, it is useful to use version control. This refers to documenting any changes to the scripts, data, or outputs. This way, it can be traced when and by whom which changes were made. It is also possible to restore previous versions of the scripts, data, or outputs.

# GitHub

[GitHub](https://github.com/) is, simply put, a cloud provider like GDrive, OneDrive, or iCloud, but specialized for code and version control. In GitHub, so-called repositories—projects can be created. These are practically the project folder, including all subfolders and files, that were introduced within the workflow. Using the application [GitHub Desktop](https://desktop.github.com/download/), a folder system on your own device can be connected to a repository on GitHub. Any changes to the files are versioned through so-called *commits* (!= saving the script). The old files are not simply overwritten; instead, it is recorded which changes exist compared to the old file. After a commit, the change can then be transferred to the repository via *push*. It is also possible to download the current state of the repository to your own device. This is called a *pull*.

# Git

The combination of a **script-based workflow** with the version control of GitHub via Git is particularly powerful. [Git](https://git-scm.com/) allows, after a one-time setup and connection to the corresponding GitHub repository, to perform commits, pushes, and pulls directly from within the project file. This eliminates the need for an additional installation of GitHub Desktop and another user interface.

::: callout-note
You can find instructions for the one-time setup of Git, GitHub, and the connection between the two [here](https://happygitwithr.com/).
:::
